title: GDPR Data Subject Rights Response SLA Dashboard
exclusions: Identity verification pauses under Art.12(6) where the clock stops pending verification completion; formally extended requests under Art.12(3) with data subject notification.
evidence: Data subject rights SLA performance report with response time distribution, completion rates by right type, extension usage, and refusal statistics with justification categories.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.12 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.12 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.19: GDPR Data Subject Rights Response SLA Dashboard.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.19' running on index=itsm sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item" (all data subject rights requests), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Create test DSAR tickets for access, erasure, and portability rights with known submission dates; allow one to exceed the 30-day SLA; verify the dashboard correctly shows the overdue request in red status.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Create test DSAR tickets for all rights and mark them complete within 15 days; verify the dashboard shows all requests in green status with no SLA breaches recorded.

dataSources: DSAR ticketing system exports (ServiceNow, Jira), consent management platform logs, identity verification workflow events, and Art.12 response correspondence tracking records
spl:
index=itsm (sourcetype="snow:sc_req_item" OR sourcetype="snow:incident")
    (cat_item="*Subject*" OR short_description="*DSAR*" OR short_description="*data subject*" OR short_description="*erasure*" OR short_description="*rectification*" OR short_description="*portability*" OR short_description="*objection*" OR short_description="*restriction*")
| eval right_type=case(
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)access|dsar|subject.access"), "Access (Art.15)",
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)erasure|forget|deletion"), "Erasure (Art.17)",
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)rectif"), "Rectification (Art.16)",
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)portab"), "Portability (Art.20)",
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)object"), "Objection (Art.21)",
    match(lower(short_description),"(?i)restrict"), "Restriction (Art.18)",
    1=1, "Other")
| eval opened_epoch=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval closed_epoch=if(isnotnull(closed_at), strptime(closed_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), null())
| eval response_days=if(isnotnull(closed_epoch), round((closed_epoch-opened_epoch)/86400,1), round((now()-opened_epoch)/86400,1))
| eval sla_status=case(
    isnotnull(closed_epoch) AND response_days<=30, "Met",
    isnotnull(closed_epoch) AND response_days<=90, "Extended (Art.12(3))",
    isnotnull(closed_epoch) AND response_days>90, "BREACHED",
    isnull(closed_epoch) AND response_days>25, "AT_RISK",
    1=1, "In Progress")
| stats count by right_type, sla_status
| chart sum(count) by right_type, sla_status
description: Article 12 requires controllers to respond to data subject rights requests "without undue delay and in any event within one month," extendable by two months for complex requests with explanation to the data subject. While UC-22.1.2 focuses on DSARs specifically, this use case provides an executive dashboard across all rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection) with SLA tracking, volume trends, and cost-per-request metrics — the compliance KPI view that DPOs and boards need.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:incident, snow:sc_req_item. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Standardise ServiceNow catalog items or incident categories for each GDPR right type; (2) train intake teams to classify correctly; (3) schedule daily; (4) alert on AT_RISK requests approaching 30-day deadline; (5) report monthly on volume trends, SLA compliance rates, and average response times by right type. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:incident`, `sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item`.
visualization: Stacked bar chart (rights by SLA status), Single value (overall SLA compliance %), Table (at-risk requests), Line chart (monthly volume trend by right type), Pie chart (requests by right type). Key fields: `closed_epoch`, `opened_epoch`, `response_days`, `right_type`, `sla_status`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Clock pauses during identity verification** — The response clock pauses while the organisation verifies the data subject's identity under Art.12(6); the dashboard may show elapsed calendar time without accounting for the pause.

2. **Art.12(3) extension applied** — Complex requests may be extended by two additional months with notification to the data subject; the dashboard should distinguish between standard and extended timelines.

3. **Manifestly unfounded or excessive requests** — Requests that have been legitimately refused under Art.12(5) may appear as overdue if the refusal is not reflected in the status field.

4. **Multi-right requests** — A single communication exercising multiple rights (access + erasure + portability) may show different completion dates for each component, creating partial-completion appearances.

5. **System downtime during processing** — Scheduled system maintenance or unplanned outages may delay automated fulfilment steps; verify against the incident log for legitimate processing interruptions.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`gdpr_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.12`. The control objective is: "Evidence that GDPR Art.12 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.19: GDPR Data Subject Rights Response SLA Dashboard." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:sc_req_item`, `snow:incident`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Standardise ServiceNow catalog items or incident categories for each GDPR right type. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** train intake teams to classify correctly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** schedule daily. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** alert on AT_RISK requests approaching 30-day deadline. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** report monthly on volume trends, SLA compliance rates, and average response times by right type. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "GDPR Data Subject Rights Response SLA Dashboard," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the GDPR control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This detection correlates event arrival time with a business timestamp from `opened_at` (format: `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S`). The `strptime()` conversion to epoch is the linchpin — the subsequent `where` clause compares Splunk's `_time` against the parsed epoch to detect policy violations (activity after a termination, events past a retention boundary, operations outside a maintenance window, etc).

Silent failure mode: if `opened_at` arrives in a format the `strptime` call doesn't match, the conversion returns null, propagates through the `where` comparison as 'unknown,' and the row is dropped from results. This produces under-reporting, not over-reporting — which is the dangerous direction for a compliance detection. Validate by running:

```spl
<base search>
| eval opened_at_epoch = strptime(opened_at, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| where isnull(opened_at_epoch)
| stats count by opened_at
```

If this returns rows, the upstream system has multiple timestamp formats (common with SaaS connectors that change during version upgrades) and the `strptime` call must be hardened with `coalesce(strptime(opened_at, "fmt1"), strptime(opened_at, "fmt2"))` to handle them all.

Time-zone alignment: if the source system emits `opened_at` in a different zone from the Splunk search head, the comparison will be offset. Look for `%z` or `%Z` in the format string — if absent, the conversion uses `TZ = TZ of Splunk host`, which is correct only when source and target agree. Search covers the full search range.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.1.19: GDPR Data Subject Rights Response SLA Dashboard]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.1.19,reg=GDPR
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `closed_epoch`, `opened_epoch`, `response_days`, `right_type`, `sla_status`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:sc_req_item earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table closed_epoch, opened_epoch, response_days, right_type, sla_status
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `response_days`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(response_days) as p50 perc90(response_days) as p90 perc99(response_days) as p99 max(response_days) as hi
```

The current threshold `30` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.1.19: GDPR evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar Chart (`rights by SLA status`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`overall SLA compliance %`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'GDPR current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Drill-through table (`at-risk requests`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 4 — Line Chart (`monthly volume trend by right type`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 5 — Pie chart (`requests by right type`):** Shows proportion across categorical dimension. Use sparingly — pie charts are poor at small-slice comparison; a bar chart is often clearer.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Silent under-reporting:** The `strptime` conversion is likely failing on a subset of events — see Step 3 for the hardening procedure. Check for format drift after upstream system upgrades.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**GDPR-specific failure modes:**

• Lawful basis drift: a detection that worked when the processing activity had consent as its lawful basis becomes misleading when the business team silently changes the RoPA (Record of Processing Activities) entry to 'legitimate interest.' Every GDPR compliance search should reference the RoPA entry ID in its comments and re-review the detection when the RoPA is revised — at minimum annually.

• Data Subject Rights timers: Article 12(3) requires response to access/erasure/portability requests within one month. Detections that measure DSR fulfilment must use business-day clocks (not wall-clock 30 days) for regulators that enforce the distinction; the threshold value in the search should align with the local Supervisory Authority's interpretation, not the ISO 8601 literal.

• Cross-border transfer fragility: Schrems II invalidated the EU-US Privacy Shield in 2020 and the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) replaced it; any detection involving transfer monitoring must key off the current adequacy decision list, not a hardcoded country allowlist. Maintain `gdpr_adequacy_list.csv` and refresh when the EDPB updates the adequacy register.

• Breach notification 72-hour window (Art. 33): the clock starts at awareness, not at the forensically-confirmed moment of compromise. Detections must capture the first-awareness event (SOC alert acknowledgment, first ticket open, first email notification) — not just the incident-resolved timestamp — or the notification SLA metric will understate breaches that took 72h+1h to confirm.
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of data subject rights response sla dashboard — so we can show we look after private information the way the privacy rules that apply to us require.
